story:
Murad smokes with the cigarette clamped in the corner of his mouth, a ring flashing on his finger, a scar on his cheek, a look that makes the world narrow to his pain. We drink; he talks about remembering himself from before two, about a mother who banned pacifiers and a father who threw an apple. The waiter keeps glancing at his ring. When Murad finally asks what I remember from childhood—“Seven, half-empty building”—I tell him the one thing I never tell.

I was seven, newly moved into a half-empty building and restless with pride about becoming a first-grader. The yard had a slide and a sandbox I deemed beneath me. One blazing day I lingered outside and, coming in late, met a man in a bright shirt on the ground floor. He smiled like a neighbor, said he was doing renovations, asked if I’d like to look. I knew not to go anywhere with strangers, but he was inside our building; neighbors felt safe. He said his name was Nico. I told him mine and stepped over the threshold.

The apartment was big and bare. He pointed—bed here, chandelier there—speaking as if he belonged. “I loved hiding in wardrobes as a kid,” he said, “a room within a room. Adults can’t fit.” He said the owners would make a children’s room. I told him I watched cartoons and was going to school soon, as if that made me older. On the windowsill stood a horsetail plant, oddly alive in an empty space. He wiped its leaves with a damp cloth, then lifted me by the waist so I could finish the last two.

He led me to a closed interior door. “Behind this is the most interesting thing. Are you afraid?” I wasn’t. He tried key after key in an embedded lock I’d never seen inside an apartment. None fit. He grew impatient. “What’s in there?” I asked. “What’s valuable can’t be easy to reach,” he said. We never opened it.

He walked me to the hall. I ran home to tell everything, expecting admiration. My mother slid into a chair; my father bolted out of the apartment. Later their friends arrived, smoke and questions hanging in the kitchen. I heard that the place I’d visited belonged to those friends—they’d bought it, hadn’t hired anyone, hadn’t started any work. No one knew a Nico.

Back at the table with Murad, I watch his fingers while my hands tremble. He asks what the memory did to me—if I fear repairmen or empty rooms. I say I forgot it for years. We moved soon after. Only at university, dusting the leaves of a horsetail left on our dorm windowsill, did the whole scene return at once—not in fragments, but intact, as if it had been waiting behind its own locked door. “In the secret room of your subconscious,” Murad says.

We ride in silence. At my entrance he asks, as always, to come up; I refuse, as always. “Why can I never come in?” he calls from the taxi window. “Because I don’t sleep with married men,” I say, waving him off. Inside, I whisper the truth I never admit to anyone: I don’t sleep with anyone.
